method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island's Japanese name is Hashima (端島), while its better-known nickname is Gunkanjima (軍艦島), commonly translated into English as “Battleship Island”.[3]  
The nickname combines the Japanese words gunkan, meaning “warship”, and jima, meaning “island”.[3]  
It originated from the island's appearance from a distance, as its compact outline and tall concrete buildings made it resemble a battleship, particularly the Japanese battleship Tosa.[2][3]

==History==
Coal use in the area preceded industrial development: residents of nearby Takashima were said to have collected coal from exposed beds as household fuel, with a local legend attributing the discovery of its combustibility to a man named Goheita.[5] Coal was first found on Hashima around 1810, and before large-scale development the island was associated with small-scale coal mining and occasional use by local fishermen.[2][1] Permanent industrial settlement expanded from 1887, as workers mined seams extending beneath the seabed.[2][3] Mitsubishi later developed Hashima as an offshore coal-mining settlement focused on undersea extraction.[4]

By the early 20th century Hashima had become an intensely built industrial community, with mining infrastructure and housing increasingly covering the island in reinforced concrete.[1][5] In 1916, when annual coal production was about 150,000 tons and the population had risen to more than 3,000, Mitsubishi built a reinforced-concrete apartment block to address housing shortages and reduce typhoon damage.[5] The building has been described as Japan’s first reinforced-concrete structure of significant size.[5] The island subsequently developed a dense urban environment of apartment blocks, industrial facilities and services for miners and their families.[1][3]

Hashima’s population reached its peak in 1959, when 5,259 residents lived on the small island.[4] The settlement remained active until the decline of coal mining led to the closure of the mine in 1974.[2][4] After the closure, residents rapidly left and the island became uninhabited.[3][4] Its abandoned concrete buildings and mining structures later became a prominent example of Japan’s industrial ruins.[1][4]

In 2015 Hashima was included as part of the UNESCO-listed Sites of Japan’s Meiji Industrial Revolution.[3][4] The listing and interpretation of the island’s past have remained subjects of debate and diplomatic tension.[1]

==Access==
Visitors can reach Hashima only on authorized guided boat tours, most of which depart from Nagasaki Port.[1][2] Nagasaki Port is accessible from Nagasaki Eki-mae by tram to Ohato Station, while buses from Nagasaki Airport to Ohato take about 40 minutes.[1] Landing on the island is restricted by Nagasaki City regulations, which require wave heights below 0.5 metres, wind speeds below 5 metres per second, and visibility of more than 500 metres.[1] Even when these conditions are met, the boat captain may cancel a landing if it is judged unsafe, and landings are possible on only about 100 days per year.[1] Because the island is in an exposed offshore location, tour departures and landings are not fully guaranteed, and visitors are advised to allow for schedule changes or cancellations.[2] Access on the island is limited to designated visitor routes, and tourists are not permitted to freely enter the apartment blocks or mining structures.[2]

==Current status==
Hashima is currently presented chiefly as an industrial-heritage and ruins-tourism site, with interpretation on visits described as emphasizing the everyday lives of miners, their families, and the former island community through photographs and views of the abandoned buildings.[1] Critics argue that this presentation gives little or no attention to the mine’s wartime use of forced labour or to the colonial and military context in which some labourers worked there.[1][4]

The island’s World Heritage status remains contested, especially by South Korea, which has argued that Japan has not fully carried out its pledge to explain the wartime conscription of Koreans at the site.[5] Japan stated during the inscription process that it would take measures to allow understanding that Koreans and others were brought against their will and forced to work under harsh conditions at some of the industrial sites in the 1940s.[4] At a later World Heritage Committee session, delegates declined South Korea’s request to reopen the Hashima issue as a formal UNESCO agenda item and instead treated the dispute as one to be addressed through bilateral dialogue.[5]

Conservation and interpretation debates therefore focus not only on preserving the island’s deteriorating industrial ruins, but also on whether its public history should cover the Meiji, Taishō, and Shōwa periods more fully, including wartime labour conditions.[1] Some commentators and researchers have questioned whether the island, as presently interpreted, adequately represents the Meiji industrial heritage for which it was inscribed, because many of the visible remains and much of the available social history relate to later periods.[1]

==World Heritage Site approval controversy==
During Japan’s nomination of its Meiji-era industrial sites, South Korea objected that seven of the 23 proposed locations were connected to wartime forced labour and should be excluded from the bid.[1] Korean researchers argued that these locations were central to the nomination while also representing unresolved histories of Japanese colonial rule, with Kim Min-chul of Kyung Hee University saying the sites were treated in Japan as “glorious” industrial heritage despite their forced-labour associations.[1]  

Hashima was among the most contested sites because Korean accounts described hundreds of Koreans working in its undersea coal mines during the war; one contemporary report stated that about 600 Koreans worked there and that many were believed to have died from exposure or overwork.[1] Japanese and Korean diplomats held talks in Tokyo over how the experiences of Korean workers would be described, reflecting wider disputes over Japan’s wartime memory and colonial-era historical interpretation.[1]  

Japanese heritage advocates argued that the nomination’s main historical focus was the period from 1850 to 1910, while the wartime mobilization of Koreans in the 1940s fell outside that chronological frame.[5] After approval, criticism continued over whether Japan had adequately presented the history of Korean labourers, and the Industrial Heritage Information Centre became a focal point of the dispute between Japan and South Korea.[5]  

In June 2020, shortly after the centre opened to the public, South Korean foreign minister Kang Kyung-wha wrote to UNESCO director-general Audrey Azoulay arguing that its explanations concerning people from the former Korean Peninsula were insufficient.[5] Kang also asked UNESCO to consider removing Hashima’s World Heritage status and called for greater cooperation from Japan over the site’s interpretation.[5] UNESCO later planned to send an inspector to the centre after discussions with Japan, while its managing director, Koko Kato, said she wanted UNESCO to judge the matter using accurate information and described the Japanese and South Korean positions as fundamentally different.[5]

==In popular culture==
Hashima Island has frequently been represented in visual media through its abandoned industrial landscape, and scholars have connected the circulation of such images to debates over collective memory, historical justice and accountability for abuses associated with the site.[1] Its profile in global popular culture increased after its appearance in the James Bond film *Skyfall*, which prompted academic discussion of how the film shaped later efforts to document and interpret the island.[1][2] One analysis argued that blockbuster uses of Hashima imagery can reinforce a celebratory view of Japan’s industrial past while displacing themes of loss, trauma and reparation into mass entertainment.[1] The island has also been linked in tourism and media writing to other productions, including the Japanese manga series *Attack on Titan*.[4] After *Skyfall*, researchers examined Google Street View imagery of Hashima as part of the island’s digital afterlife, considering how navigation, spatial presence and digital heritage influence public engagement with the ruins.[1] Hashima has also inspired practice-based artistic work, including the 2013 performance *Return to Battleship Island*, which combined creative writing and filmed image as part of the AHRC-funded project *The Future of Ruins: Reclaiming Abandonment and Toxicity on Hashima Island*.[5]
